```text
1	# shopsystem-product
2	
3	Lead shop of the **shopsystem** product, in the DDD sense: the outward face of
4	the shop system where stakeholders meet the work and where product-level
5	artifacts live. The shopsystem is itself a product built by a lead shop and
6	three BC-shops; this repo holds the lead shop's content. Rationale and the
7	four-repo split are in [`adr/001-framework-packaging.md`](adr/001-framework-packaging.md).
8	
9	## What's inside
10	
11	Framework spec (read in order):
12	
13	- [§1 Principles](01-principles.md) — six principles paired with the anti-pattern each rules out.
14	- [§2 Bounded Contexts and Subdomains](02-bounded-contexts-and-subdomains.md) — problem-space vs solution-space; recognised vs drawn.
15	- [§3 Lead shop](03-lead-shop.md) — PO and Architect roles, artifacts, and the turn-limited exchange.
16	- [§4 BC-shop](04-bc-shop.md) — Implementer and adversarial Reviewer roles, artifacts, the §4.4 loop.
17	- [§5 Inter-shop protocol](05-inter-shop-protocol.md) — channel, routing, wire format, message catalogue.
18	- [§6 Work tracking](06-work-tracking.md) — hybrid beads model and reconciliation.
19	
20	Architecture decisions:
21	
22	- [`adr/001-framework-packaging.md`](adr/001-framework-packaging.md) — split into four BC-aligned repos.
23	
24	Findings (consolidated per-prototype):
25	
26	- [`findings/from-prototype-1.md`](findings/from-prototype-1.md) — message-catalog-v1 (14 slices, 2026-05-06 → 2026-05-10).
27	- [`findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md`](findings/from-mechanism-observation-v1.md) — mechanism-observation-v1.
28	
29	Prototype evidence (frozen records — design, plan, per-slice runs):
30	
31	- [`prototypes/message-catalog-v1/`](prototypes/message-catalog-v1/)
32	- [`prototypes/mechanism-observation-v1/`](prototypes/mechanism-observation-v1/)
33	
34	## Sibling BC repos
35	
36	The other three bounded contexts of the shopsystem live alongside:
37	
38	- [shopsystem-messaging](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-messaging) — Pydantic schemas, `shop-msg` CLI, messaging scenarios.
39	- [shopsystem-scenarios](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-scenarios) — Gherkin canonicalization rule + hash + `scenarios` CLI.
40	- [shopsystem-templates](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates) — role templates (lead-po, lead-architect, bc-implementer, bc-reviewer) + `shop-templates` CLI.
41	
42	Dependency direction and per-repo responsibilities are detailed in ADR-001.
43	
44	## Provenance
45	
46	This repo originated from
47	[`github.com/dstengle/ddd-product-system`](https://github.com/dstengle/ddd-product-system).
48	The framework-internal `docs/shop-system/` directory was the lead-shop output
49	of the shopsystem under its earlier framing; ADR-001 promotes it into this
50	BC-aligned repo. The migration is tracked by ADR-001 in this repo at
51	[`adr/001-framework-packaging.md`](adr/001-framework-packaging.md).
52	
53	## License
54	
55	MIT. See [LICENSE](LICENSE).
56
```
</details></tool-use>

1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd prime` for full workflow context.
4	
5	## Quick Reference
6	
7	```bash
8	bd ready              # Find available work
9	bd show <id>          # View issue details
10	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
11	bd close <id>         # Complete work
12	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
13	```
14	
15	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
16	
17	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
18	
19	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
20	
21	**Use these forms instead:**
22	```bash
23	# Force overwrite without prompting
24	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
25	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
26	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
27	
28	# For recursive operations
29	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
30	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
31	```
32	
33	**Other commands that may prompt:**
34	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
35	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
36	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
37	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
38	
39	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION v:1 profile:minimal hash:ca08a54f -->
40	## Beads Issue Tracker
41	
42	This project uses **bd (beads)** for issue tracking. Run `bd prime` to see full workflow context and commands.
43	
44	### Quick Reference
45	
46	```bash
47	bd ready              # Find available work
48	bd show <id>          # View issue details
49	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work
50	bd close <id>         # Complete work
51	```
52	
53	### Rules
54	
55	- Use `bd` for ALL task tracking — do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown TODO lists
56	- Run `bd prime` for detailed command reference and session close protocol
57	- Use `bd remember` for persistent knowledge — do NOT use MEMORY.md files
58	
59	## Session Completion
60	
61	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
62	
63	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
64	
65	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
66	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
67	3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
68	4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
69	   ```bash
70	   git pull --rebase
71	   bd dolt push
72	   git push
73	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
74	   ```
75	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
76	6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
77	7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
78	
79	**CRITICAL RULES:**
80	- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
81	- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
82	- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
83	- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
84	<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
85
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# ADR-003 — Ecommerce as the first real-domain instantiation
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-05-11)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude
5	**Depends on:** [ADR-001](001-framework-packaging.md), [ADR-002](002-harness-bc-introduction.md)
6	
7	## Decision
8	
9	Stand up an **ecommerce product** as the first non-toy consumer of the
10	shopsystem framework. Ecommerce was the original framing the framework
11	was extracted from (per ADR-001's provenance note); using it as the
12	validation target tests the framework against a domain it was implicitly
13	designed for, but never previously instantiated end-to-end.
14	
15	The instantiation is staged:
16	
17	| Stage | Scope | Validates |
18	|---|---|---|
19	| 1 (this ADR) | Lead shop + one BC | Framework round-trips with real product intent (not toy `bc-shop`/temperature) |
20	| 2 (future ADR) | Lead shop + two BCs with one cross-BC contract | Prototype-1 §8 #1 (multi-BC topology, cross-BC fan-out) |
21	| 3+ (future ADRs) | Add BCs as real use cases demand | Cumulative pressure on the framework |
22	
23	Stage 1 is the work this ADR commits to. Stages 2+ are sequencing
24	intent, not commitments.
25	
26	## Stage 1 — what gets built
27	
28	A new lead-shop repo `ecommerce-product` and one BC-shop repo
29	`ecommerce-catalog`. Naming follows ADR-001's convention: no "shop"
30	suffix; org/product prefix carries the structural context.
31	
32	**Why Catalog as the first BC**:
33	
34	- It's the most self-contained ecommerce BC — products, descriptions,
35	  prices, availability flags. Doesn't depend on Cart, Checkout, or
36	  Customer.
37	- It has a clear customer-facing contract (the public product list /
38	  product detail). Real product intent has a natural home here.
39	- It surfaces no transactional or PII-sensitive concerns at stage 1,
40	  keeping the validation exercise unencumbered.
41	
42	**What `ecommerce-product` (the lead shop) holds**:
43	
44	- Product brief: a one-page statement of what the ecommerce product is
45	  for. Authored by the PO (the human driver in this stage).
46	- ADR-003 (this file, copied into the new repo): the architectural
47	  context for stage 1.
48	- Domain & Context Map skeleton: lists the Catalog subdomain and its
49	  BC. Stage 2 adds the second BC and the first cross-BC relationship.
50	- `pyproject.toml` consuming the three framework packages + the
51	  test-harness (per consumer-wiring.md).
52	- `features/catalog/`: the canonical Gherkin scenarios for the first
53	  Catalog slice.
54	
55	**What `ecommerce-catalog` (the BC-shop) holds**:
56	
57	- Code implementing whatever the first slice's scenarios pin
58	  (`src/ecommerce_catalog/`).
59	- A small CLI surface for the slice's behavior (we'll see what makes
60	  sense — possibly `catalog add`, `catalog list`).
61	- Step defs + a Gherkin runner.
62	- A `shop-card.yaml` declaring the BC's metadata.
63	
64	## Stage 1 — the first slice
65	
66	Pick **one** vertical slice of catalog behavior that exercises the
67	framework end-to-end:
68	
69	> *"As a catalog operator, I can register a product with a name, price,
70	> and availability flag, and list all currently-available products."*
71	
72	This yields ~3-5 scenarios:
73	
74	1. Register a product with valid attributes; list shows it.
75	2. Register a product whose availability flag is false; list omits it.
76	3. Register two products; list returns them in stable order.
77	4. Listing an empty catalog returns no rows and exits 0.
78	
79	The slice is mechanically uninteresting (the implementation is a list
80	in memory or a SQLite file). The validation is **the dispatch and
81	review loop**: lead shop PO authors → Architect dispatches via
82	`shop-msg send assign_scenarios` → BC Implementer adds CLI + step
83	defs → BC Reviewer probes → work_done → reconciliation. If this loop
84	runs cleanly against real product intent (not framework-internal
85	backfill), Stage 1 has succeeded.
86	
87	## What this validates that prior work didn't
88	
89	Everything to date has been **framework-internal** — the lead shop's
90	work item was always to extend or pin the framework itself. Stage 1 is
91	the first time real product intent flows through the framework's
92	mechanisms. The risks Stage 1 exposes:
93	
94	- **Brief-to-scenarios gap.** The PO has to translate a real product
95	  brief into Gherkin without the prior "the implementation already
96	  exists" anchor. Whether the lead-po role template is sufficient for
97	  this is unvalidated.
98	- **Architect choices on a clean slate.** The Architect picks which
99	  scenarios to assign in which order. No existing BC test layer is
100	  there to anchor pre-state. The lead-architect template's
101	  message-type discriminator hasn't been exercised on a virgin BC.
102	- **BC implementer creativity.** The implementer must invent code, not
103	  just pin existing behavior. Sufficiency check #4 ("does BC already
104	  implement this?") returns NO genuinely for the first time outside
105	  the harness slice.
106	
107	## Stage 1 — sequence
108	
109	1. Author this ADR (draft, this commit).
110	2. Create `ecommerce-product` GitHub repo. README + LICENSE +
111	   `pyproject.toml` + `bd init`. Set up beads remote
112	   `ecommerce-product-beads` (private).
113	3. Author a one-page product brief at `ecommerce-product/brief.md`
114	   (PO role; human-driven).
115	4. Create `ecommerce-catalog` GitHub repo. Same scaffolding. Beads
116	   remote.
117	5. Use the test harness (`shop-test-harness bootstrap`) to validate
118	   the inter-shop wiring works in an isolated topology before
119	   committing the production directories.
120	6. Lead PO authors 3-5 scenarios for the first slice.
121	7. Lead Architect dispatches `assign_scenarios` to `ecommerce-catalog`.
122	8. BC Implementer implements + Reviewer gates.
123	9. Reconcile; record findings in `ecommerce-product/findings/`.
124	
125	## What this defers
126	
127	- **Stage 2+ BCs**. Cart, Checkout, Customer, Inventory — all out of
128	  scope until Stage 1 closes.
129	- **Persistent storage choices**. Whether the catalog BC backs to
130	  SQLite, Postgres, an in-memory store, or something else is a Stage
131	  1 implementation detail and may be reconsidered each slice.
132	- **HTTP / API surface**. Stage 1 uses CLI subcommands. A web API
133	  surface (and the BC that owns it) is a future concern.
134	- **Real users**. The PO and Architect for Stage 1 are the human
135	  driver. No actual ecommerce stakeholders are interviewed at this
136	  stage. Stage 1's purpose is framework validation, not market
137	  validation.
138	
139	## Open questions
140	
141	1. **Should the first slice be `catalog add` + `catalog list`, or
142	   should it be the read-only "list available products" half only?**
143	   Going read-only-first keeps the slice smaller. Going write+read
144	   exercises a fuller round-trip but risks scope creep.
145	2. **Where does the ecommerce-product lead shop's brief come from?**
146	   The framework assumes stakeholder intent. With no real stakeholder,
147	   the PO writes a brief that's plausible but not externally
148	   anchored. Is this still a meaningful validation of the
149	   brief-to-scenarios path?
150	3. **Do we name `ecommerce-product`'s GitHub org under `dstengle`
151	   alongside `shopsystem-*`, or in a different org?** Naming
152	   convention (ADR-001) makes this cosmetic; the question is whether
153	   the visual coupling is helpful or noisy.
154	
155	## What we'd learn from Stage 1
156	
157	A small number of *generative* findings would justify Stage 1
158	regardless of whether the catalog behavior is interesting:
159	
160	- Whether the lead-po template handles a virgin brief or if it needs
161	  template additions for "first slice from a blank brief."
162	- Whether the message-type discriminator (`assign_scenarios` vs
163	  `request_bugfix` vs `request_maintenance`) is intuitive for the
164	  Architect when no BC code exists.
165	- Whether the harness's `bootstrap/freeze/verify` actually helps an
166	  experiment-level validation, or is over-/under-scoped for real
167	  product work.
168	- Whether the dogfooding hash-flow (lead beads ID → scenario hash →
169	  work_done → reconciliation) works the same when production code is
170	  on the other end.
171	
172	If any of those reveals a real gap, ADR-004 captures the response.
173
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# ADR-002 — Introducing the harness Bounded Context
2	
3	**Status:** accepted (2026-05-11)
4	**Authors:** dstengle, Claude
5	**Depends on:** [`adr/001-framework-packaging.md`](001-framework-packaging.md)
6	
7	## Decision
8	
9	Add a fifth repository, `shopsystem-test-harness`, holding a Bounded Context whose
10	job is to drive experiments and validate the shopsystem framework against
11	itself. Per §2.3, the harness BC belongs to a self-introduced **Platform
12	Operations** subdomain — it serves the product itself rather than the
13	product's end users.
14	
15	| Repo | BC | Subdomain | UL focus |
16	|---|---|---|---|
17	| `shopsystem-product` | (lead shop) | — | product-level artifacts |
18	| `shopsystem-messaging` | Messaging | Inter-shop coordination | message, work_id, schema, wire format |
19	| `shopsystem-scenarios` | Scenarios | Specification | scenario, canonical, hash, tag |
20	| `shopsystem-templates` | Templates | Role discipline | role, template, dispatch, sufficiency check |
21	| `shopsystem-test-harness` (new) | Harness | **Platform Operations** | experiment, slice, run, evidence, finding, baseline |
22	
23	## Why a BC and not just tooling
24	
25	The harness has a coherent internal model that the other three BCs don't
26	share and shouldn't have to learn:
27	
28	- An **experiment** is a named investigation with a target question.
29	- A **slice** is a discrete unit of work within an experiment — inputs,
30	  expected outcomes, a name.
31	- A **run** is one execution of a slice (a slice can have multiple runs).
32	- **Evidence** is the frozen artifacts produced by a run: inboxes,
33	  outboxes, dispatch reports, before/after code states.
34	- A **finding** is a load-bearing claim that emerges from one or more
35	  runs, with evidence pointers.
36	- A **baseline** is a known-good state the harness validates against.
37	
38	This vocabulary doesn't fit cleanly inside any of the other three packages.
39	Bundling it into `shopsystem-messaging` or `shopsystem-templates` would mix
40	ULs — the exact anti-pattern ADR-001 ruled out for the original split.
41	
42	## Scope (in)
43	
44	- Bootstrap an isolated topology: a temporary directory containing fake
45	  lead-shop and BC-shop directories with their own inboxes, outboxes, and
46	  beads instances.
47	- Define and execute slices: invoke role templates against work items,
48	  capture stdout/stderr, freeze the resulting filesystem state.
49	- Forward + reverse conformance checks against a slice's expected
50	  outcomes.
51	- Findings aggregation: walk recorded runs, produce a `findings.md`
52	  matching the prototype convention.
53	
54	## Scope (out — explicitly)
55	
56	- **Not** a scaffolding tool for *production* shop creation. Standing up a
57	  new real BC-shop for a consumer product is a separate concern (likely
58	  belongs in `shopsystem-templates` or a thin scaffolding CLI).
59	- **Not** the inter-shop transport. Slices use the same `shop-msg` /
60	  `scenarios` / `shop-templates` surface as production work — the harness
61	  composes them, it doesn't replace them.
62	- **Not** a CI runner. The harness is invoked manually during framework
63	  development; if CI calls it, fine, but the harness has no opinion about
64	  CI.
65	
66	## Dependencies
67	
68	```
69	shopsystem-test-harness → shopsystem-messaging (composes shop-msg)
70	                  → shopsystem-scenarios  (composes scenarios)
71	                  → shopsystem-templates  (composes shop-templates)
72	```
73	
74	All composition is at the CLI boundary per Prototype-1 Finding 6
75	(package boundaries hold under dogfooding pressure).
76	
77	## Initial CLI surface (proposed)
78	
79	```
80	shop-test-harness init <experiment-name>
81	shop-test-harness slice new <slice-name>
82	shop-test-harness bootstrap                    # spin up isolated topology
83	shop-test-harness dispatch <role> <work-id>    # invoke a role template
84	shop-test-harness freeze                       # capture run as evidence
85	shop-test-harness verify                       # conformance checks
86	shop-test-harness findings list
87	```
88	
89	CLI names are draft — eligible for refinement during initial slices, same
90	as `shop-msg` / `scenarios` / `shop-templates` were during prototype-1.
91	
92	## Initial scenarios (the BC's own pinning, ~8)
93	
94	1. `bootstrap creates isolated lead-shop directory with beads`
95	2. `bootstrap creates isolated BC-shop with inbox + outbox`
96	3. `dispatch invokes a role template with the work_item as context`
97	4. `dispatch captures stdout, stderr, and exit code as artifacts`
98	5. `freeze writes a complete evidence directory for the run`
99	6. `verify passes when all assigned scenarios appear in the register`
100	7. `verify fails with diff when assigned scenario is missing`
101	8. `findings list walks recorded runs and returns load-bearing claims`
102	
103	These are draft titles. The actual Gherkin gets authored by the lead shop
104	(PO role) once the BC exists, and dispatched to `shopsystem-test-harness` via
105	`assign_scenarios` — dogfooding the introduction.
106	
107	## Decided
108	
109	- **Repo name.** `shopsystem-test-harness`.
110	- **CLI name.** `shop-test-harness`.
111	- **Evidence storage.** Keep the prototype `runs/N/` convention. Human-
112	  readable evidence is more valuable than content-addressing at this scale.
113	
114	## Deferred
115	
116	1. **Role-template versioning.** When the harness exercises a template
117	   that has since changed, can it pin a template version? Defer until a
118	   slice surfaces the need.
119	2. **Multi-BC topology slices.** Prototype-1 §8 #1 (cross-BC fan-out) is
120	   still unvalidated. The harness should make this *cheap* enough that
121	   a slice can stand up N=3+ BCs without ceremony.
122	
123	## Sequencing relative to other open work
124	
125	Per the broader plan (this is the third of four threads after beads init
126	and scenario backfill):
127	
128	1. ✅ Beads init across the four existing repos.
129	2. ⏳ Backfill scenarios for `shopsystem-scenarios` and `shopsystem-templates`
130	   so their behavior is design-pinned before the harness exercises them.
131	3. **This ADR**: create `shopsystem-test-harness` repo, scaffold, dispatch its
132	   initial scenarios from the lead shop.
133	4. Real-domain instantiation: **ecommerce** as the first consumer
134	   product. Stands up a non-trivial product BC using the complete
135	   framework + harness; will be tracked by a separate ADR (ADR-003)
136	   when sequencing reaches that stage.
137	
138	The harness's own scenarios get dispatched via `assign_scenarios` from
139	the lead shop after the repo is created — the harness BC's introduction
140	is itself a dogfooding instance.
141	
142	## What this defers
143	
144	- The harness's full slice catalogue (built incrementally).
145	- Cross-BC fan-out slices (the highest-leverage unvalidated item from
146	  prototype-1 §8 #1) — first real workload for the harness once it exists.
147	- Real-product validation (ecommerce — ADR-003 when ready). Depends on
148	  the harness being stable enough to validate the consumer's first
149	  BC-shop end-to-end.
150
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./03-lead-shop.md`

```text
1	# §3 Lead shop
2	
3	The lead shop is the outward face of the shop system — where stakeholders meet the work. It owns all product-level artifacts, coordinates the BC-shops, and houses the two roles responsible for product intent and product shape. The shop system is composed of one lead shop and one or more BC-shops; the lead shop is the single point through which intent enters and reconciliation returns. There is exactly one lead shop per product.
4	
5	## 3.1 Roles
6	
7	**Product Owner (PO).** The PO is stakeholder-facing and owns product intent — translating expressed desire into requirements artifacts (the product brief, PDRs, Gherkin scenarios) that the rest of the shop system can act on. The PO holds the authoritative picture of what the product is supposed to do and is the named party for scope and vocabulary questions arriving from BC-shops via `clarify`. When a BC-shop asks whether a term means one thing or another, or whether a scenario is in or out of scope, the PO is the party who answers. The PO does not design structure — that is the Architect's concern — but the PO does author the Gherkin scenarios that structure the work, since scenarios are requirements before they are assignments.
8	
9	**Architect.** The Architect owns product shape, scenario assignment, and reconciliation. Where the PO answers *what*, the Architect answers *how it is structured* — maintaining the structurizr workspace, decomposing the problem into Bounded Contexts, assigning scenarios to the right BC-shop, and closing the loop when work comes back. The Architect is the party that sends `assign_scenarios`, `request_bugfix`, and `request_maintenance` to BC-shops, and that pulls and interprets scenario registers to verify that assigned work has landed. The two roles collaborate on BC decomposition, with a hard cap on rounds to keep that exchange bounded (see [§3.4](#34-turn-limited-po--architect-exchange) for the turn-limit mechanics). The Architect is also the named party for BC-shop `clarify` messages on architecture — questions about structure, contracts, or decomposition decisions.
10	
11	## 3.2 Activities
12	
13	The activities of the lead shop divide cleanly between the two roles: the PO works upstream (stakeholders → requirements → scenarios) while the Architect works downstream (scenarios → assignments → reconciliation). The two roles meet at BC decomposition, which is a bounded collaboration with a shared output — the Domain & Context Map. The table below is the authoritative activity catalogue.
14	
15	| Role | Activity | Artifacts touched |
16	|---|---|---|
17	| PO | Interview stakeholder | interview notes |
18	| PO | Maintain product brief | brief |
19	| PO | Write PDR for new functionality | PDR |
20	| PO | Write Gherkin scenarios as requirements | scenarios (with tags + stable hash) |
21	| PO | Respond to BC `clarify` (scope, vocabulary) | clarification reply |
22	| Architect | Write ADRs | ADR |
23	| Architect | Maintain structurizr workspace (containers, components, dynamic views) | structurizr DSL |
24	| Architect | Collaborate with PO on BC decomposition (turn-limited) | Domain & Context Map |
25	| Architect | Assign scenarios to BCs per structurizr | scenario-to-BC assignment, lead beads issues, `assign_scenarios` |
26	| Architect | Reconcile scenario registers against assigned work | `request_scenario_register`, lead beads close-out |
27	| Architect | Send `request_bugfix` / `request_maintenance` | lead beads issues, message |
28	| Architect | Read a BC-shop's card via `request_shop_card` | message, BC-shop card |
29	| Architect | Respond to BC `clarify` (architecture) | clarification reply |
30	
31	## 3.3 Artifacts owned
32	
33	The lead shop is the heavier side of the artifact split — it holds all product-level artifacts, while each BC-shop holds only what it needs to do its own work. The list below names each lead-shop-owned artifact with its format and its one-clause job.
34	
35	- **Product brief** *(Markdown)* — the authoritative statement of what the product is and what it is for; the entry point for stakeholder intent.
36	- **PDRs** *(Markdown)* — Product Decision Records; records of product-level decisions, each anchored to a point of intent expressed by the PO.
37	- **ADRs** *(Markdown)* — Architecture Decision Records; records of structural decisions made by the Architect; govern how the product is decomposed and how BCs relate.
38	- **Structurizr workspace** *(Structurizr DSL)* — the canonical structural model of the product, holding static diagrams (containers, components) and dynamic views; the Architect's primary instrument for BC decomposition.
39	- **Domain & Context Map** *(YAML; schema deferred to a future prototype — see [findings/from-prototype-1.md §8](findings/from-prototype-1.md))* — the bridge between problem space (subdomains) and solution space (Bounded Contexts); records subdomain-to-BC assignments and cross-BC relationships. Owned jointly by both roles; PO and Architect produce it through their bounded collaboration.
40	- **Gherkin scenarios** *(Gherkin; tags carry stable hash + owning BC)* — requirements expressed as executable specifications, authored by the PO; each scenario carries tags that hold a stable hash and the owning BC, making assignment and reconciliation unambiguous. The lead shop holds the canonical copy; BC-shops receive their assigned scenarios inline via `assign_scenarios` and hold local copies alongside the implementation.
41	- **Scenario-to-BC assignment** *(YAML; schema deferred to a future prototype — see [findings/from-prototype-1.md §8](findings/from-prototype-1.md))* — the explicit mapping from each Gherkin scenario to the BC-shop responsible for implementing it; produced by the Architect from the structurizr workspace and used to drive `assign_scenarios` messages.
42	- **Lead beads** *(beads native)* — the canonical work registry for the shop system; one lead beads issue per assigned scenario, bugfix, or maintenance unit, with IDs that flow outward into inter-shop messages (see [§6](06-work-tracking.md)). Work IDs are always lead beads IDs.
43	- **Shop card** *(YAML; schema deferred to a future prototype — see [findings/from-prototype-1.md §8](findings/from-prototype-1.md))* — the lead shop's own declarative metadata: name, roles, activities offered. Lives at a known path in the lead shop's repo (e.g. `shop-card.yaml`); served via the message channel if requested.
44	
45	## 3.4 Turn-limited PO ↔ Architect exchange
46	
47	When PO and Architect collaborate on BC decomposition, the exchange is bounded:
48	
49	- **Hard cap of 3 rounds by default.**
50	- Either party may request **one extension**, which the other accepts or refuses.
51	- Refusal forces accept-current. The current state of the Domain & Context Map at that point is recorded as the agreed decomposition.
52	
53	The cap and extension mechanics are the same shape as ordinary inter-shop messages even though both parties live in the same shop.
54	
55	## 3.5 Cross-references
56	
57	- Inter-shop messages and routing: see [§5](05-inter-shop-protocol.md).
58	- Work tracking model and beads usage: see [§6](06-work-tracking.md).
59	- BC-shop side of the relationship: see [§4](04-bc-shop.md).
60	- Empirical validation of the lead shop's role split, message-type discipline, and scenario-assignment flow: see [`findings/from-prototype-1.md`](findings/from-prototype-1.md).
61
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./04-bc-shop.md`

```text
1	# §4 BC-shop
2	
3	A BC-shop produces one Bounded Context of the product. The relationship between BC and shop is one-to-one: one shop = one repo = one BC, and the BC name is the shop name. The BC-shop receives its work from the lead shop via the inter-shop protocol, carries out implementation and review internally, and reports back when work is complete.
4	
5	## 4.1 Roles
6	
7	**Implementer.** The Implementer receives assigned work from the lead shop, opens a top-level beads issue that cross-references the lead beads ID, and breaks the work down into sub-issues. The Implementer writes the code and tests, runs the BDD suite to confirm assigned scenarios pass, and updates the scenario register accordingly. When scope or vocabulary is unclear, the Implementer sends a `clarify` message to the lead shop rather than making assumptions. Treating "clarity" as self-evident is unreliable — a capable agent will fill gaps from BC code or world knowledge unless its role is equipped with explicit criteria for what counts as a sufficient request. Those criteria live in the BC-shop's role template (system prompt or analysis skill), not in this spec, so they can be iterated without spec churn. The Implementer cannot emit `work_done` unilaterally — that gate is held by the Reviewer.
8	
9	**Reviewer.** The Reviewer's stance is adversarial by design. The Reviewer probes the implementation against the assigned scenarios, looking for cases the Gherkin does not cover and for implementation behaviour that violates intent even when tests pass. Where the Implementer's job is to make things work, the Reviewer's job is to find where they break. The Reviewer records findings in beads and blocks `work_done` until satisfied. When the Reviewer judges that a scenario is too loosely specified to catch the failure mode found, the finding loops back to the lead shop via `clarify` for the PO to decide on — it is not the Reviewer's call to rewrite requirements unilaterally.
10	
11	## 4.2 Activities
12	
13	The Implementer handles the mechanics of receiving work, building, and reporting; the Reviewer handles the adversarial quality gate. The two roles are sequential within a unit of work: implementation proceeds until the Reviewer's gate opens. The table below is the authoritative activity catalogue for the BC-shop.
14	
15	| Role | Activity | Artifacts touched |
16	|---|---|---|
17	| Implementer | Receive work, open top-level beads issue with lead ID as external ref | beads issue |
18	| Implementer | Break down into sub-issues | beads sub-issues |
19	| Implementer | Implement (code + tests) | code, tests |
20	| Implementer | Run BDD suite, update scenario register | scenario register |
21	| Implementer | Send `work_done` | message |
22	| Implementer | Send `clarify` when scope/vocabulary unclear | message |
23	| Implementer | Maintain shop card and respond to `request_shop_card` | shop card |
24	| Reviewer | Adversarial probe of implementation against assigned scenarios | review notes (in beads) |
25	| Reviewer | Construct failing cases not covered by Gherkin | edge-case findings |
26	| Reviewer | Gate `work_done` (block until satisfied) | beads state |
27	| Reviewer | Propose scenario tightenings back to lead shop via `clarify` | message |
28	
29	## 4.3 Artifacts owned
30	
31	The BC-shop holds only what it needs to do its own work — implementation artifacts and the local tracking layer. The list below names each BC-shop-owned artifact with its format and its one-clause job.
32	
33	- **Code, tests, build artifacts** *(language-specific)* — the primary product of the BC-shop; held in the BC repo.
34	- **Local Gherkin scenarios** *(Gherkin)* — received from the lead shop inline via `assign_scenarios` and written to the features directory on receipt; maintained alongside the implementation as the local reference for what the BC is expected to do.
35	- **Scenario register** *(YAML; schema deferred to a future prototype — see [findings/from-prototype-1.md §8](findings/from-prototype-1.md))* — the list of Gherkin scenario hashes currently passing in this BC-shop; the Implementer updates it after each BDD run and the lead shop pulls it during reconciliation via `request_scenario_register`.
36	- **BC-shop beads** *(beads native)* — the local breakdown of assigned work; one top-level issue per unit of work received, cross-referencing the lead beads ID as an external reference, with sub-issues for the Implementer's internal breakdown.
37	- **Shop card** *(YAML; schema deferred to a future prototype — see [findings/from-prototype-1.md §8](findings/from-prototype-1.md))* — this BC-shop's own declarative metadata: name, BC, roles, activities offered. Lives at a known path in the BC repo (e.g. `shop-card.yaml`); served to the lead shop via `request_shop_card`.
38	
39	## 4.4 Reviewer mechanics
40	
41	The Reviewer functions as an internal gate — an adversarial checkpoint that sits between the Implementer finishing and the BC-shop reporting back to the lead shop. The gate is not advisory: the Implementer cannot emit `work_done` until the Reviewer signs off, and that sign-off is recorded as a state transition on the top-level beads issue. The lead shop sees only `work_done`; the internal blocking is invisible to it, which is intentional — the BC-shop is responsible for its own quality boundary.
42	
43	When the Reviewer's probing surfaces a failure mode that the existing Gherkin would not catch — a scenario whose specified behaviour is too loose to rule out the defect — the finding does not stay inside the BC-shop. The Reviewer proposes a scenario tightening back to the lead shop via `clarify`. The PO receives the `clarify` and decides whether to tighten the Gherkin. If the PO accepts the tightening, the revised scenario returns to the BC-shop as a `request_bugfix` carrying the tightened scenario text. The BC-shop then implements against the tighter specification and the Reviewer re-gates.
44	
45	This loop — Reviewer finds gap → `clarify` → PO decides → `request_bugfix` with tightened scenario → BC-shop re-implements → Reviewer re-gates — is the mechanism by which discovered edge cases feed back into the canonical requirements rather than living only in ad hoc test code. It keeps the Gherkin corpus honest over time.
46	
47	The loop reuses existing message types — `clarify`, `request_bugfix`, `work_done` — rather than introducing §4.4-specific machinery. Implementations should not invent special handling for it; the role-template architecture and the message catalog produce the loop as a natural consequence of holding the gate.
48	
49	A second outcome category surfaces in practice: **tighten without code change**. When the Reviewer's probing finds that the implementation already handles a case but no scenario pins it, the right §4.4 result is to add the pinning scenario and verify (typically via counterfactual: would removing the existing handler break the new test?), without modifying production code. This is distinct from `work_done(blocked)` — the implementation isn't wrong, the requirements just weren't tight enough to prevent future regression.
50	
51	## 4.5 Cross-references
52	
53	- Lead shop side: [§3](03-lead-shop.md).
54	- Inter-shop messages and routing: [§5](05-inter-shop-protocol.md).
55	- Work tracking and beads cross-references: [§6](06-work-tracking.md).
56	- Empirical validation of the role-template architecture, the §4.4 loop (three full closures), and the schema-vs-template-vs-tool discriminator: see [`findings/from-prototype-1.md`](findings/from-prototype-1.md).
57
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./repos/shopsystem-test-harness/README.md`

```text
1	# shopsystem-test-harness
2	
3	Harness Bounded Context of the **shopsystem** product. Per
4	[ADR-002](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-product/blob/main/adr/002-harness-bc-introduction.md)
5	this BC sits in a self-introduced **Platform Operations** subdomain — it
6	serves the product itself rather than the product's end users.
7	
8	## What it's for
9	
10	Driving experiments and validating the shopsystem framework against
11	itself. The harness composes the three framework packages
12	(`shopsystem-messaging`, `shopsystem-scenarios`, `shopsystem-templates`)
13	at the CLI boundary (per prototype-1 finding 6) to spin up isolated
14	shop topologies, dispatch role templates against work items, capture
15	artifacts as evidence, and aggregate findings across runs.
16	
17	## What it isn't
18	
19	- **Not** a scaffolding tool for *production* shop creation. Standing up
20	  a real BC-shop for a consumer product is a separate concern.
21	- **Not** the inter-shop transport. Slices use the same `shop-msg`,
22	  `scenarios`, `shop-templates` surface as production work.
23	- **Not** a CI runner. The harness is invoked manually during framework
24	  development; CI can call it, but it has no opinion about CI.
25	
26	## Ubiquitous language
27	
28	- **experiment** — a named investigation with a target question.
29	- **slice** — a discrete unit of work within an experiment.
30	- **run** — one execution of a slice.
31	- **evidence** — frozen artifacts produced by a run (inboxes, outboxes,
32	  reports, before/after states).
33	- **finding** — a load-bearing claim emerging from one or more runs,
34	  with evidence pointers.
35	- **baseline** — a known-good state the harness validates against.
36	
37	## CLI surface
38	
39	The CLI binary is `shop-test-harness`. Subcommands are added as the
40	behavior gets pinned by scenarios. See `features/` for the current
41	contract.
42	
43	## Dependencies
44	
45	```
46	shopsystem-test-harness → shopsystem-messaging
47	                       → shopsystem-scenarios
48	                       → shopsystem-templates
49	```
50	
51	All composition is at the CLI boundary.
52	
53	## License
54	
55	MIT. See [LICENSE](LICENSE).
56
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:00:01Z)**_

1	"""Discovers Gherkin features under shopsystem-test-harness/features/
2	and registers them as pytest-bdd scenarios. No-op when no .feature
3	files are present, so the suite stays green during a future
4	drop-everything reset.

6	Mirrors the same pattern shopsystem-scenarios uses for its BDD
7	discovery.
8	"""

10	
11	from pytest_bdd import scenarios
12	
13	_features_dir = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "features"
14	if any(_features_dir.glob("*.feature")):
15	    scenarios(str(_features_dir))

1	"""shop-test-harness CLI entry point.
2	
3	Subcommands are added as behavior gets pinned by scenarios under
4	``features/``. This module's job is purely the argparse wiring; per-
5	subcommand handlers live in sibling modules and are imported here.
6	
7	Subcommands wired:
8	
9	* ``bootstrap`` — create a lead-shop + BC-shop topology under --target
10	  (``shop_test_harness.bootstrap``).
11	* ``freeze`` — copy a live topology into ``<evidence>/runs/N/``
12	  (``shop_test_harness.freeze``).
13	* ``verify`` — compare echoed scenario hashes against an expected list
14	  (``shop_test_harness.verify``).
15	"""
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import argparse
19	import sys
20	
21	from shop_test_harness import bootstrap, freeze, verify
22	
23	
24	def build_parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
25	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
26	        prog="shop-test-harness",
27	        description=(
28	            "Harness for the shopsystem product. Drives experiments "
29	            "and validates the framework against itself by composing "
30	            "shop-msg, scenarios, and shop-templates at the CLI "
31	            "boundary."
32	        ),
33	    )
34	    subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command")
35	
36	    bootstrap.add_subparser(subparsers)
37	    freeze.add_subparser(subparsers)
38	    verify.add_subparser(subparsers)
39	
40	    return parser
41	
42	
43	def main(argv: list[str] | None = None) -> int:
44	    parser = build_parser()
45	    args = parser.parse_args(argv)
46	    if args.command is None:
47	        parser.print_help(sys.stderr)
48	        return 0
49	    handler = getattr(args, "handler", None)
50	    if handler is None:
51	        # Should not happen — every subparser sets ``handler`` as a default.
52	        parser.print_help(sys.stderr)
53	        return 2
54	    return handler(args)
55	
56	
57	if __name__ == "__main__":
58	    sys.exit(main())
59
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	"""``shop-test-harness bootstrap`` — stand up an isolated shop topology.
2	
3	A bootstrapped topology under ``--target`` is two sibling shop
4	directories: the lead shop (``<target>/<lead-shop>/``) and one BC shop
5	(``<target>/<bc-shop>/``). Each shop is the same shape downstream
6	tooling (``shop-msg``, ``bd``) expects:
7	
8	    <shop>/
9	      inbox/
10	      outbox/
11	      .beads/        (initialized via ``bd init``)
12	
13	The two scenarios pinned in ``features/`` require that:
14	
15	* both subdirectories exist with empty ``inbox/`` and ``outbox/``;
16	* both have a ``.beads/`` directory whose beads metadata is sufficient
17	  for ``bd list`` to run cleanly, with the issue prefix matching the
18	  shop name;
19	* ``shop-msg send request_maintenance --bc-root <target>/<bc-shop>``
20	  succeeds against the resulting BC shop.
21	
22	We delegate beads metadata creation to ``bd init`` itself (rather than
23	synthesizing ``.beads/config.yaml`` by hand) — that is the only sure
24	way to keep up with whatever the bd binary considers "sufficient".
25	``--skip-agents --skip-hooks`` keep the init free of editor/agent
26	artifacts that would just be noise in an experiment evidence trail;
27	``--non-interactive`` makes it safe under pytest's captured stdin.
28	"""
29	from __future__ import annotations
30	
31	import argparse
32	import os
33	import subprocess
34	import sys
35	from pathlib import Path
36	
37	
38	def _make_shop(shop_dir: Path, prefix: str) -> None:
39	    """Create one shop's directory layout and initialize beads in it.
40	
41	    Idempotent on the directory layout (``mkdir`` with ``exist_ok``)
42	    but defers to ``bd init`` for the ``.beads/`` contents. ``bd init``
43	    is invoked with the shop directory as its working directory so the
44	    embedded Dolt database lands inside the shop, not in the harness
45	    process's cwd.
46	    """
47	    (shop_dir / "inbox").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
48	    (shop_dir / "outbox").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
49	
50	    # Hand the inner directory to ``bd init``. The ``--prefix`` flag
51	    # sets the issue-id prefix, which the scenarios require to match
52	    # the shop directory name.
53	    env = os.environ.copy()
54	    env["BD_NON_INTERACTIVE"] = "1"
55	    subprocess.run(
56	        [
57	            "bd",
58	            "init",
59	            "--prefix",
60	            prefix,
61	            "--quiet",
62	            "--skip-agents",
63	            "--skip-hooks",
64	            "--non-interactive",
65	        ],
66	        cwd=str(shop_dir),
67	        env=env,
68	        check=True,
69	        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
70	        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
71	    )
72	
73	
74	def run_bootstrap(
75	    target: Path,
76	    lead_shop: str,
77	    bc_shop: str,
78	) -> int:
79	    """Create a fresh ``lead-shop`` + ``bc-shop`` topology under ``target``.
80	
81	    Returns the process exit code (0 on success). Errors from ``bd init``
82	    propagate as a non-zero exit with the underlying stderr forwarded.
83	    """
84	    target = target.resolve()
85	    target.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
86	
87	    try:
88	        _make_shop(target / lead_shop, lead_shop)
89	        _make_shop(target / bc_shop, bc_shop)
90	    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as exc:
91	        sys.stderr.write(
92	            f"bootstrap: bd init failed: {exc.stderr.decode() if exc.stderr else exc}\n"
93	        )
94	        return 1
95	    return 0
96	
97	
98	def add_subparser(subparsers: argparse._SubParsersAction) -> None:
99	    p = subparsers.add_parser(
100	        "bootstrap",
101	        help="create an isolated lead-shop + BC-shop topology",
102	        description=(
103	            "Create an isolated experiment topology under --target with "
104	            "two shops (lead and one BC), each containing empty inbox/ "
105	            "and outbox/ subdirectories and a .beads/ initialized via "
106	            "`bd init` with the shop name as the issue prefix."
107	        ),
108	    )
109	    p.add_argument("--target", required=True, type=Path,
110	                   help="target directory under which the topology is created")
111	    p.add_argument("--lead-shop", required=True,
112	                   help="name of the lead-shop subdirectory")
113	    p.add_argument("--bc-shop", required=True,
114	                   help="name of the BC-shop subdirectory")
115	    p.set_defaults(handler=_handler)
116	
117	
118	def _handler(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
119	    return run_bootstrap(
120	        target=args.target,
121	        lead_shop=args.lead_shop,
122	        bc_shop=args.bc_shop,
123	    )
124
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>

 `./repos/shopsystem-test-harness/src/shop_test_harness/freeze.py`

```text
1	"""``shop-test-harness freeze`` — snapshot a live topology into evidence.
2	
3	The scenario pins three things:
4	
5	1. Output directory is ``<evidence>/runs/N/`` where N is the next free
6	   integer (1, 2, 3, ...).
7	2. Every file under each shop in ``<target>/`` is mirrored under the
8	   matching path inside ``<evidence>/runs/N/``, byte-for-byte.
9	3. The result is a complete, self-contained copy of the topology at
10	   the moment freeze was called.
11	
12	We use ``shutil.copytree`` per shop subdirectory rather than copying
13	the whole target, because the scenario assertions are scoped to the
14	shop subdirectories (``<dir>/lead/`` and ``<dir>/bc-foo/``) — copying
15	the whole target would also pull anything the experiment runner has
16	lying around at the top level, which is not what evidence should
17	contain. A shop is anything under ``<target>/`` that has both
18	``inbox/`` and ``outbox/`` subdirectories.
19	
20	The embedded Dolt runtime under each shop's ``.beads/embeddeddolt/``
21	is normally gitignored. We *do* copy it: the scenario says
22	"every file under <dir>/lead/ and <dir>/bc-foo/ is present", with
23	no exception carved out for the Dolt runtime. If a future run finds
24	the runtime files corrupt the byte-for-byte guarantee under
25	concurrent bd activity, that will surface as a scenario failure and
26	the lead can carve out an exception then.
27	"""
28	from __future__ import annotations
29	
30	import argparse
31	import shutil
32	import sys
33	from pathlib import Path
34	
35	
36	def _next_run_index(runs_dir: Path) -> int:
37	    """Return the next free integer N such that ``runs_dir/N`` does not exist."""
38	    if not runs_dir.exists():
39	        return 1
40	    used: list[int] = []
41	    for child in runs_dir.iterdir():
42	        if child.is_dir() and child.name.isdigit():
43	            used.append(int(child.name))
44	    return (max(used) + 1) if used else 1
45	
46	
47	def _is_shop_dir(path: Path) -> bool:
48	    """A shop is any subdirectory of <target> with both inbox/ and outbox/."""
49	    return path.is_dir() and (path / "inbox").is_dir() and (path / "outbox").is_dir()
50	
51	
52	def run_freeze(target: Path, evidence: Path) -> int:
53	    target = target.resolve()
54	    evidence = evidence.resolve()
55	
56	    if not target.exists() or not target.is_dir():
57	        sys.stderr.write(f"freeze: target does not exist or is not a directory: {target}\n")
58	        return 1
59	
60	    shops = [child for child in sorted(target.iterdir()) if _is_shop_dir(child)]
61	    if not shops:
62	        sys.stderr.write(f"freeze: no shops (inbox/+outbox/ subdirs) found under {target}\n")
63	        return 1
64	
65	    runs_dir = evidence / "runs"
66	    n = _next_run_index(runs_dir)
67	    out_dir = runs_dir / str(n)
68	    out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=False)
69	
70	    for shop in shops:
71	        dest = out_dir / shop.name
72	        # ``copytree`` preserves the directory structure under each shop
73	        # verbatim, including dotfiles. ``dirs_exist_ok=False`` is fine
74	        # because we just created ``out_dir``.
75	        shutil.copytree(shop, dest, symlinks=True, dirs_exist_ok=False)
76	
77	    return 0
78	
79	
80	def add_subparser(subparsers: argparse._SubParsersAction) -> None:
81	    p = subparsers.add_parser(
82	        "freeze",
83	        help="copy a live topology into <evidence>/runs/N/",
84	        description=(
85	            "Copy every shop subdirectory under --target into a fresh "
86	            "<evidence>/runs/N/ directory, where N is the next free "
87	            "integer. The result is a byte-for-byte snapshot of the "
88	            "topology at the moment of the call."
89	        ),
90	    )
91	    p.add_argument("--target", required=True, type=Path,
92	                   help="experiment target directory to freeze")
93	    p.add_argument("--evidence", required=True, type=Path,
94	                   help="evidence root directory (runs/ is created under it)")
95	    p.set_defaults(handler=_handler)
96	
97	
98	def _handler(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
99	    return run_freeze(target=args.target, evidence=args.evidence)
100
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./repos/shopsystem-test-harness/src/shop_test_harness/verify.py`

```text
1	"""``shop-test-harness verify`` — compare the BC's echoed scenario hashes
2	to an expected list.
3	
4	Inputs:
5	
6	* ``--target <dir>`` — experiment target directory containing the BC shop.
7	* ``--bc <name>`` — BC shop subdirectory name.
8	* ``--work-id <id>`` — work_id identifying the assignment whose
9	  ``work_done`` is being verified.
10	* ``--expected <path>`` — file with one expected hash per line. Blank
11	  lines and lines starting with ``#`` are ignored so the file can carry
12	  a human-readable header without breaking parsing.
13	
14	Behavior:
15	
16	* On match (set equality): exit 0, stdout ``"verify: N hashes match"``,
17	  empty stderr. We report the size of the *matched* set because that is
18	  what the scenario asserts.
19	* On divergence: exit non-zero, empty stdout, stderr names two gaps
20	  separately:
21	
22	    - forward-conformance: hashes expected but not echoed back
23	      (the BC under-delivered or echoed the wrong hashes).
24	    - reverse-conformance: hashes echoed back but not expected
25	      (the BC over-delivered, claimed credit it was not assigned).
26	
27	The two labels matter to the scenario, so the wording is fixed.
28	"""
29	from __future__ import annotations
30	
31	import argparse
32	import sys
33	from pathlib import Path
34	
35	import yaml
36	
37	from catalog.schemas import WorkDone
38	
39	
40	def _load_expected(path: Path) -> list[str]:
41	    text = path.read_text()
42	    out: list[str] = []
43	    for raw_line in text.splitlines():
44	        line = raw_line.strip()
45	        if not line or line.startswith("#"):
46	            continue
47	        out.append(line)
48	    return out
49	
50	
51	def _load_work_done(target: Path, bc: str, work_id: str) -> WorkDone:
52	    """Locate and parse ``<target>/<bc>/outbox/<work-id>-work_done.yaml``."""
53	    path = target / bc / "outbox" / f"{work_id}-work_done.yaml"
54	    if not path.exists():
55	        raise FileNotFoundError(f"work_done file not found at {path}")
56	    data = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text())
57	    # Validate via the messaging package's schema rather than trusting
58	    # raw YAML — this also enforces ``message_type == 'work_done'``.
59	    return WorkDone(**data)
60	
61	
62	def run_verify(
63	    target: Path,
64	    bc: str,
65	    work_id: str,
66	    expected_path: Path,
67	) -> int:
68	    target = target.resolve()
69	    expected_path = expected_path.resolve()
70	
71	    try:
72	        work_done = _load_work_done(target, bc, work_id)
73	    except FileNotFoundError as exc:
74	        sys.stderr.write(f"verify: {exc}\n")
75	        return 2
76	    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — surface schema errors verbatim
77	        sys.stderr.write(f"verify: failed to parse work_done: {exc}\n")
78	        return 2
79	
80	    expected = set(_load_expected(expected_path))
81	    echoed = set(work_done.scenario_hashes)
82	
83	    forward_missing = expected - echoed   # expected but not echoed
84	    reverse_extra = echoed - expected     # echoed but not expected
85	
86	    if not forward_missing and not reverse_extra:
87	        matched = len(expected & echoed)
88	        sys.stdout.write(f"verify: {matched} hashes match\n")
89	        return 0
90	
91	    # Divergence path: name both gaps separately, in deterministic order.
92	    if forward_missing:
93	        for h in sorted(forward_missing):
94	            sys.stderr.write(
95	                f"forward-conformance gap: expected but not echoed: {h}\n"
96	            )
97	    if reverse_extra:
98	        for h in sorted(reverse_extra):
99	            sys.stderr.write(
100	                f"reverse-conformance gap: echoed but not assigned: {h}\n"
101	            )
102	    return 1
103	
104	
105	def add_subparser(subparsers: argparse._SubParsersAction) -> None:
106	    p = subparsers.add_parser(
107	        "verify",
108	        help="check that a BC's work_done echoed back the expected scenario hashes",
109	        description=(
110	            "Compare scenario_hashes echoed back in a BC shop's "
111	            "<work-id>-work_done.yaml against an expected list of "
112	            "hashes (one per line). Exits 0 on set equality. On "
113	            "divergence, exits non-zero and reports forward-conformance "
114	            "(expected-but-not-echoed) and reverse-conformance "
115	            "(echoed-but-not-assigned) gaps separately on stderr."
116	        ),
117	    )
118	    p.add_argument("--target", required=True, type=Path,
119	                   help="experiment target directory containing the BC shop")
120	    p.add_argument("--bc", required=True,
121	                   help="BC shop subdirectory name")
122	    p.add_argument("--work-id", required=True,
123	                   help="work_id whose work_done is being verified")
124	    p.add_argument("--expected", required=True, type=Path,
125	                   help="path to file listing expected scenario hashes "
126	                        "(one per line; blanks and # comments ignored)")
127	    p.set_defaults(handler=_handler)
128	
129	
130	def _handler(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
131	    return run_verify(
132	        target=args.target,
133	        bc=args.bc,
134	        work_id=args.work_id,
135	        expected_path=args.expected,
136	    )
137
```

338	def _files_under(root: Path) -> dict[str, bytes]:
339	    """Return {relpath -> bytes} for every regular file under ``root``.
340	
341	    Symlinks are followed if they resolve to a file; otherwise skipped.
342	    This is the byte-for-byte comparison primitive used by the freeze
343	    Then.
344	    """
345	    out: dict[str, bytes] = {}
346	    for path in root.rglob("*"):
347	        if path.is_file():
348	            rel = path.relative_to(root).as_posix()
349	            out[rel] = path.read_bytes()
350	    return out

355	        'every file under "<dir>/{lead_shop}/" and "<dir>/{bc_shop}/" is '

358	    )
359	)
360	def then_every_file_present(
361	    lead_shop: str, bc_shop: str, n: int, context: dict
362	) -> None:

365	    run_dir = evidence / "runs" / str(n)
366	    for shop_name in (lead_shop, bc_shop):
367	        src = target / shop_name
368	        dst = run_dir / shop_name
369	        assert dst.is_dir(), f"expected snapshot dir {dst} to exist"
370	        src_files = _files_under(src)
371	        dst_files = _files_under(dst)
372	        # A missing file is more diagnostic than a generic dict-equality
373	        # failure, so check membership + content explicitly.
374	        missing = set(src_files) - set(dst_files)
375	        assert not missing, (
376	            f"files missing from snapshot of {shop_name}/: {sorted(missing)}"
377	        )
378	        for rel, body in src_files.items():
379	            assert dst_files[rel] == body, (
380	                f"byte mismatch in snapshot of {shop_name}/{rel}"
381	            )

384	@then(

388	def then_evidence_self_contained(context: dict) -> None:
389	    """Sanity: deleting the source target leaves the evidence intact and
390	    still readable. This pins "self-contained" rather than "happens to
391	    share inodes" — i.e. it would fail if freeze used hardlinks or
392	    symlinks back to the source.
393	    """
394	    target: Path = context["target"]
395	    run_dir: Path = context["run_dir"]
396	    # Snapshot what's there first.
397	    before = _files_under(run_dir)
398	    # Now destroy the source.
399	    shutil.rmtree(target)
400	    after = _files_under(run_dir)
401	    assert before == after, (
402	        "evidence is not self-contained: deleting the source changed "
403	        "its contents"
404	    )

412	def _write_work_done(
413	    target: Path, bc: str, work_id: str, hashes: list[str]
414	) -> Path:
415	    """Drop a valid ``<work-id>-work_done.yaml`` into the BC's outbox.
416	
417	    We use ``shop-msg respond work_done`` so the file shape exactly
418	    matches what a real BC would write — going through the CLI is
419	    cheaper than synthesizing the YAML by hand and lying about its
420	    provenance.
421	    """
422	    bc_root = target / bc
423	    (bc_root / "inbox").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
424	    (bc_root / "outbox").mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
425	    cmd = [
426	        "shop-msg", "respond", "work_done",
427	        "--bc-root", str(bc_root),
428	        "--work-id", work_id,
429	        "--status", "complete",
430	    ]
431	    for h in hashes:
432	        cmd.extend(["--scenario-hash", h])
433	    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
434	    assert result.returncode == 0, (
435	        f"setup: shop-msg respond work_done failed: {result.stderr}"
436	    )
437	    return bc_root / "outbox" / f"{work_id}-work_done.yaml"

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./consumer-wiring.md`

```text
1	# Consumer wiring (phase 1)
2	
3	How a consumer product or a BC-shop pulls in the shopsystem framework
4	packages in phase-1, per [ADR-001](adr/001-framework-packaging.md)
5	§Phase-1 wiring.
6	
7	## What the consumer depends on
8	
9	The three framework packages live under separate repos but use plain
10	distribution names. The repo name and distribution name diverge for
11	two of the three; declare the dep by **distribution name** on the left
12	of `@`:
13	
14	| Repo                                                                | Distribution name      |
15	|---------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------|
16	| [shopsystem-scenarios](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-scenarios)   | `scenarios`            |
17	| [shopsystem-templates](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates)   | `shop-templates`       |
18	| [shopsystem-messaging](https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-messaging)   | `shopsystem-messaging` |
19	
20	`shopsystem-messaging` transitively pulls `scenarios` (the messaging
21	schemas delegate the canonical scenario-hash to `scenarios.hash`), so
22	a consumer that needs messaging doesn't need to declare `scenarios`
23	explicitly — but declaring it anyway is the more robust pattern
24	(explicit > transitive when the consumer reads the dep list).
25	
26	## `pyproject.toml` snippet
27	
28	```toml
29	[project]
30	name = "my-bc-shop"
31	requires-python = ">=3.10"
32	dependencies = [
33	    "scenarios @ git+https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-scenarios@v0.1.0",
34	    "shop-templates @ git+https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-templates@v0.1.0",
35	    "shopsystem-messaging @ git+https://github.com/dstengle/shopsystem-messaging@v0.1.0",
36	]
37	```
38	
39	Pin to a **tag**, not a branch. The shape stabilizes through tags;
40	phase-2 will publish to PyPI once names are settled.
41	
42	## Install and verify
43	
44	```bash
45	pip install -e .                  # or `pip install .` for non-dev
46	shop-msg --help                   # messaging CLI on PATH
47	scenarios --help                  # scenarios CLI on PATH
48	shop-templates list               # 4 role templates
49	```
50	
51	A lead-shop consumes all three. A BC-shop typically consumes
52	`shopsystem-messaging` (for `shop-msg` + `catalog.schemas`) and
53	`shop-templates` (for the BC role prompts); `scenarios` arrives
54	transitively.
55	
56	## What stays editable
57	
58	Nothing — phase-1 explicitly moves consumers off editable installs of
59	the framework packages. Editable installs remain appropriate for the
60	consumer's own packages (the lead-shop and BC-shop directories within
61	the consumer product's repo), not for the framework deps.
62	
63	## What this defers (phase-2)
64	
65	- **PyPI publishing.** When shapes stabilize, framework packages will
66	  publish, and consumers will drop the `@ git+...` form for plain
67	  version specifiers.
68	- **CLI renames.** The CLIs keep their current binary names
69	  (`shop-msg`, `scenarios`, `shop-templates`) per ADR-001's
70	  no-rename-in-phase-1 rule.
71
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:03:17Z)**_

ADR-003 is parked on `adr-003-ecommerce-draft`, main is cleaned up. Here's the rest:

## Cleanup summary

| Did | Why |
|---|---|
| Branched `adr-003-ecommerce-draft` from `27852a8`, reverted ADR-003 on main | Preserves draft without keeping it in the shopsystem product's history |
| Fixed README — dropped dead `prototypes/` links, added ADR-002 + the `features/` index | README pointed at paths removed in `da12819`, and didn't list the canonical PO-authored scenarios |

The `ADR-003` reference in `adr/002-harness-bc-introduction.md` is forward-looking ("when ready") and stays valid — the draft just isn't on main yet.

Things I noticed but didn't touch:
- `findings/from-prototype-1.md` cites `prototypes/message-catalog-v1/runs/…` paths that no longer exist locally. These are frozen records — rewriting them falsifies history. The evidence lives in `ddd-product-system` (now noted in the README). Consider adding an explicit pointer at the top of the findings doc instead.
- `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` overlap heavily. Merging would be tidier but they serve different agent ecosystems — defer.
- No open beads issues anywhere in this repo or the harness repo. The harness work that ADR-002 promised (`dispatch`, `findings list`, multi-BC topology) has no tracking.

## Harness assessment

**What works today** (verified: 5/5 scenarios pass with `PATH=/workspaces/shopsystem-product/.venv/bin:$PATH pytest`):

- `shop-test-harness bootstrap --target <dir> --lead-shop <l> --bc-shop <b>` → spins up one lead + one BC, each with `inbox/`, `outbox/`, `.beads/` (correct prefix). Issue: hardcoded to exactly one BC.
- `shop-test-harness freeze --target <dir> --evidence <e>` → byte-exact snapshot at `<e>/runs/N/`. Self-contained (deleting source doesn't perturb evidence).
- `shop-test-harness verify --target <dir> --bc <b> --work-id <w> --expected <f>` → forward/reverse conformance on `work_done.yaml` scenario hashes.

These are good primitives but they only let you assert *static structural invariants*. They don't let you exercise the framework's **dynamic** behavior — the loop is the thing that matters.

**The load-bearing gap: `dispatch`**

ADR-002 lists `shop-test-harness dispatch <role> <work-id>` as a target — it's not implemented. Without it, you can't replay the inter-shop loop (assign_scenarios → BC implements → work_done → reconcile) inside a quarantined topology. That loop is the framework's whole point; not being able to run it in a slice means harness "validation" today is structural only.

**What I'd build to actually validate the system + move fast**, in order:

1. **`dispatch <role> <shop> <work-id>`** — invoke a role template (subagent or rendered prompt) against a work item in the named shop. Capture stdout/stderr/exit + file diffs into the run's evidence. This is the one primitive that unlocks everything else.
2. **A canonical smoke slice** — one toy assign_scenarios → implement-stub → work_done → verify round-trip, ~10-30s to run, with deterministic fixtures. This becomes the regression gate every framework PR has to pass.
3. **`diff <run-A> <run-B>`** — compare two frozen runs. Without diff, you can freeze but can't tell *what changed*. Pair with a "baseline" tag (a blessed run pointer) so the smoke slice is `bootstrap + dispatch + freeze + diff vs baseline`.
4. **Multi-BC topology** — relax `--bc-shop` to `--bc-shop` (repeatable) so cross-BC fan-out (Prototype-1 §8 #1, currently unvalidated) becomes cheap to slice.
5. **`findings list`** — walk recorded runs and emit load-bearing claims with evidence pointers. Lowest priority; can wait until enough runs exist to be worth aggregating.

**The "move fast" loop this enables**: any framework-touching change in `shopsystem-messaging`/`-templates`/`-scenarios` runs the smoke slice locally (or in CI) → diff vs baseline → red diff = regression caught before it touches a production shop. New features stage as a new harness slice first; you only graduate to production once the slice is stable. The harness becomes the framework's outer test loop.

**Side notes / bugs to note**:
- `verify.py:37` imports `from catalog.schemas import WorkDone` — that resolves because `shopsystem-messaging` exposes a `catalog` package, but the import line is opaque (you'd expect `shopsystem_messaging.something`). Consider re-exporting from the messaging package's namespace; otherwise leave a one-line comment about the provenance.
- The 5 `bc:…` and `scenario_hash:…` Gherkin tags throw `PytestUnknownMarkWarning`. Register them in `pyproject.toml`/`pytest.ini` (`markers = ["scenario_hash: …", "bc: …"]`) to clear noise.

## Recommended next move

If you want me to file beads issues for items 1-5 (one per primitive) so the work is trackable, say the word and I'll do it in the harness repo. Otherwise this is where the assessment lands.

Status: ADR-003 branched + revert pushed, README fix pushed, main is up to date.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-12 15:05:06Z)**_

Short answers:

**My prompt** is the system prompt for Claude Code, Anthropic's CLI. It tells me how to behave as an interactive coding agent — tone, tool-use discipline, when to ask before destructive actions, how to use beads, how to manage memory, and so on. It also pulls in your project's `CLAUDE.md` (the beads workflow + session-close protocol) and the `AGENTS.md` content from the harness repo when I read files there. It does not contain product/domain instructions specific to shopsystem.

**Does it instruct me on how to create the dispatch feature?** **No.** Nothing in my prompt says anything about `dispatch`, role templates, or how to invoke them. What I wrote in the previous turn was my own synthesis from reading:

- `adr/002-harness-bc-introduction.md` (which *lists* `dispatch <role> <work-id>` in the proposed CLI surface but doesn't specify it)
- `03-lead-shop.md` and `04-bc-shop.md` (roles, activities, the §4.4 loop)
- The current harness source (`bootstrap`, `freeze`, `verify`) — which establishes the *shape* (argparse subcommand, run, capture artifacts, return exit code) that a sibling `dispatch` module would follow
- The harness's own scenarios (the testing/pinning convention)

So the "build dispatch" recommendation is a reading of the existing artifacts + the gap they imply, not a recipe I was handed. The actual design of `dispatch` — what a "role template" invocation concretely is (a subagent call? a rendered prompt against the role template file? something that shells out to `shop-templates show <role>` and pipes it to an LLM?), what artifacts get captured, what the input contract looks like — is **not** specified anywhere I've read. That'd be the lead shop's call: the PO writes scenarios pinning the behavior, the Architect dispatches them to the harness BC, and the harness implements against the scenarios. The framework's own discipline applies to building the framework.

If you want, I can sketch a one-page design proposal for `dispatch` — input/output contract, what gets captured as evidence, and 3-5 Gherkin scenarios that would pin it — and you can decide whether it matches your intent before any code gets written.
